meta.title: Global Supply Chain Transmission
meta.subtitle: Five chains · Four control states for the Strait of Hormuz · Five-stage worst-case timeline · Country political fragility · Cross-chain feedback

source_report.title: Global supply chain exposure under Iran regime scenarios

meta.stance_summary:
**Hormuz is currently in a mixed-mode operational state** — some ships paying tolls, others being cyclically detained, the US Navy escorting selectively, and the marine insurance market retreating in tiers. **68 fully laden tankers are stranded in the Persian Gulf**, holding roughly 16 billion liters of crude. Brent is in the $80–95 band.

**The US Navy 5th Fleet is the key hard constraint** — escorting a 5–10 ship convoy requires 8–10 destroyers, and **even at full effort the Navy can restore only 10% of pre-war Hormuz flow**. Hegseth publicly calls the escort posture "a gift to the whole world," but CNN's May coverage flatly reports "why US warships will not escort commercial vessels" — the political messaging diverges sharply from operational reality.

**Iran has now empirically tested the red line**: the March 18, 2026 coordinated attack on Qatar's Ras Laffan, Saudi SAMREF, and Kuwaiti and UAE energy infrastructure. Qatar expelled Iran's military attaché; Saudi Arabia publicly stated it "reserves the right to take military action against Iran." Trump publicly extended deterrence — "the US will act if Qatar is hit again." The Gulf Trio has reversed from the "low hostility" of the 2023 Beijing reconciliation to **de facto hostility** — but the form their opposition takes is **opposition to the kind of escalation that would drag them in**, **not opposition to Iran being weakened** (on which point they are in fact aligned with Israel's strategic goals).

**Global supply chains transmit through five chains + one hard constraint**: oil & gas / marine insurance / defense industry / semiconductors / financial plumbing + US Navy capacity. Semiconductors are the most counterintuitive but most critical chain — Israel's fab cluster (Intel 18A / NVIDIA Mellanox / Tower Semi) all sits inside Iranian missile range; TSMC's LNG buffer is just 7 days, and any disruption to Qatari exports forces immediate output cuts.

**The seven most dangerous countries**: Pakistan (6–12 months), Egypt (6–12 months), Sri Lanka (12–18 months), Bangladesh (12–18 months), Jordan (12–24 months), Turkey (12–24 months, with new PKK risk), Japan (no collapse, but political pivot).

chains.name: Oil & gas
chains.thesis: Hormuz carries roughly 30% of global seaborne oil and 20% of LNG each day. Iran's regime state determines the Strait's operational mode, which in turn sets the oil price path.
chains.current_baseline: Brent benchmark at $70–80/barrel (baseline date 2026-05-19). Wartime peaks touched $110; the post-ceasefire range has settled into $75–85.
chains.scenarios_impact: **Maintained**: oil $75–90, moderate volatility · **Militarization**: oil $90–110, sustained elevated range · **Slow-collapse**: gradual upward drift · **Disintegration + blockade**: oil spikes to $180–220, exceeding the relative magnitude of the 1973 crisis
key_data: 30% of global seaborne oil transits Hormuz (IEA data)
20% of global LNG transits Hormuz (chiefly Qatar, UAE, Oman)
Iran's own oil production roughly 2.5 mb/d pre-war (2026), halved during wartime
Khuzestan fields hold 80% of Iran's reserves; loss of control under disintegration removes ~1.5 mb/d from global supply

sources.label: CRS R45281: Iran Conflict and the Strait of Hormuz
Atlantic Council, March 2026: Tehran's Toll Booth

chains.analysis:
The Strait of Hormuz is the world's most critical energy chokepoint. EIA 2024 data: roughly 21 mb/d of seaborne oil pass through the strait each day (28–30% of global seaborne crude), along with about 130 bcm/year of LNG (20% of global seaborne LNG). No bypass can fully substitute — Saudi Arabia's East-West Pipeline can divert only 5 mb/d around the strait, the UAE's Habshan-Fujairah line just 1.5 mb/d. Together that is less than a third of Hormuz throughput.

Iran's weaponization of strait passage rights has never meant a full closure — it means selective disruption. CRS R45281 documents the historical pattern: during the 1980–88 Tanker War both sides attacked each other's commercial shipping over 500 times yet the strait was never fully closed; in 2019 the IRGC seized the British tanker Stena Impero in Hormuz waters for 14 days; in 2024 the MSC Aries (Israeli-linked) and other vessels were detained. The Atlantic Council's March 2026 report Tehran's Toll Booth documents a new mode — $2 million per ship in passage fees — that shifts the use of passage rights from "embargo" to "toll-collection," a far more sustainable posture.

Under the maintained path oil settles back into $75–90. Under disintegration plus prolonged closure it spikes to $180–220, exceeding the relative magnitude of the 1973 shock. This is consistent with the EIA's 2024 extreme-scenario multiplier estimate of roughly 2.4×.

On the maintained path, Iran extracts roughly $73 billion a year through the "$2 million per ship" model, based on IRGC navy estimates of 100 tankers per day — comparable to Iran's pre-war oil export revenue at 1.5 mb/d. This is the migration of geographic rent from "exporting our own oil" to "taxing everyone else's." Under the disintegration path, a Khuzestan uprising could reach Saudi Aramco's East-West pipeline terminus and the Khorramshahr valve station — knocking out the combined Saudi + UAE bypass capacity (4.7 mb/d), and pushing the global energy system into an "irreplaceable" state.

China is the most exposed to Hormuz transit (40% of its crude imports route through it), but via the shadow fleet plus 95% of Iran-China bilateral trade settled through mBridge (per Atlantic Council Toll Booth data), China's actual discount on Iranian crude may deepen to $30–40/barrel below Brent — a discount no oil buyer has commanded since the Cold War.

chains.name: Shipping insurance
chains.thesis: War risk insurance is a precondition for transiting any chokepoint. Once private markets retreat, sovereign backstops (DFC and counterparts) become the only option.
chains.current_baseline: Lloyd's of London plus the International Group of P&I clubs still write Persian Gulf transit cover, but premiums have moved from a pre-war 0.02–0.05% of cargo value to 0.5–1.5%. Hatzadony (May 2026) labels Iran's naval "toll" the Insurance Weapon.
chains.scenarios_impact: **Maintained / Militarization**: premiums elevated but the market still functions · **Disintegration + blockade**: private market fully retreats; only sovereign-backed shipping (the DFC's $40B facility plus a Chinese counterpart) keeps moving; global shipping falls to 5–10% of pre-war volume
key_data: DFC (US Development Finance Corporation) stands up a $40B sovereign reinsurance facility (WEF 2026 report)
Global dry bulk and container shipping in the Persian Gulf and Red Sea are hit simultaneously
Suez Canal revenue has already fallen from $900M–1B/month in 2023 to $400–500M/month (post-2024 Red Sea attacks); a Hormuz closure on top of that drives it to near zero

sources.label: Hatzadony, Small Wars Journal, May 2026: The Insurance Weapon

chains.analysis:
Marine insurance is the supply chain that does not show up on the map. Lloyd's of London and the International Group of P&I clubs (an alliance of 13 mutual associations) cover roughly 90% of the world's commercial fleet for liability, with reinsurance markets (Munich Re, Swiss Re) bearing the tail. War risk is an add-on — vanishingly cheap in peacetime (0.02–0.05% of cargo value), jumping to 0.5–1.5% in wartime.

Hatzadony's May 2026 Small Wars Journal essay The Insurance Weapon makes the point sharply: Iran does not need to formally close Hormuz. It only needs to inject enough uncertainty (frequent ship seizures, partial toll collection, unpredictable attacks) to disable insurance pricing. Once pricing fails, the next step is uninsurability — operators cannot get cover, which functions as a de facto blockade.

The WEF's 2026 report describes the response: the DFC's $40 billion sovereign reinsurance facility steps in when the private market retreats, letting trade keep moving. This is the financial-era equivalent of Operation Earnest Will, the US convoy operation during the 1980–88 Tanker War. China is standing up a similar national insurance consortium, operating in the open by 2026.

The result: private insurance markets withdraw permanently from geopolitical hotspots, and sovereign-backed shipping becomes the new normal — one of the most structural supply-chain legacies of the Iran war.

The WEF's April 2026 report puts the judgment up front: "governments are not just stabilizing markets. They are increasingly underwriting them." This is the largest direct US government intervention in the global shipping insurance market since the Great Depression.

Lloyd's CEO John Neal publicly announced a $20 billion DFC-Chubb backstop in March 2026 (Insurance Journal, March 19, 2026, Tiernan cover). JPMorgan's energy team estimates 329 vessels are operating in the Persian Gulf and require about $352 billion in insurance coverage — capacity the private market no longer provides.

Hatzadony's paper documents a 60-fold rise in P&I premium costs during wartime; the LMA's rebuttal report finds 88% of market participants still willing to underwrite. Both are right: the willingness is there, but the price is unaffordable for shipowners.

chains.name: Defense industry
chains.thesis: US munitions burn for Israel has effectively gutted Indo-Pacific deterrence — the first DPA-compelled industrial mobilization since the 1950 Korean War.
chains.current_baseline: US PrSM, THAAD and Patriot interceptor consumption is around 45–50% (CSIS Cancian 2026). Lockheed Martin and Raytheon capacity expansions in Arkansas, Alabama and Texas are being accelerated under DPA (Defense Production Act) authority.
chains.scenarios_impact: **In every scenario** the US defense industrial base enters expansion; the difference is duration. **In the worst case** the Taiwan Strait window is hollowed out for 5–8 years and the probability of Chinese action against Taiwan rises 2–3 fold
key_data: US forces have consumed 45% of PrSM, ~50% of THAAD and ~50% of Patriot interceptors (depletion as a share of inventory)
Iran fired 1,500+ ballistic missiles in six months (roughly half its pre-war 2,500–6,000 stockpile, per RAND/Cohen)
PAC-3 and THAAD deliveries to Taiwan, Japan and Korea have slipped 18–36 months
Indo-Pacific deterrence effectively hollowed out for 5–8 years

sources.label: CSIS Cancian, April 2026: Last Rounds — Status of Key Munitions

chains.analysis:
US defense industrial capacity has gone through its largest transformation since the 1950 Korean War over 2024–2026. The core numbers from Mark Cancian's April 2026 CSIS report Last Rounds: Status of Key Munitions at the Iran War Ceasefire:

US munitions resupply to Israel has consumed 45% of the PrSM (Precision Strike Missile) inventory, 50% of THAAD interceptors and 50% of Patriot interceptors — these are depletion rates as a share of stockpile, not absolute volumes. RAND's Cohen separately documents Iran burning through 1,500+ ballistic missiles in six months (about half of its pre-war 2,500–6,000 stockpile), with US-side interceptor resupply to Israel far exceeding pre-war planning assumptions.

The response has been a capacity surge. Lockheed Martin (PrSM) and Raytheon (Patriot, SM-series) plant expansions across Arkansas, Alabama and Texas have been accelerated under DPA (Defense Production Act) authority. This is the first peacetime DPA-driven industrial mobilization since the law was passed in 1950 — a quasi-wartime economy.

The price is that Indo-Pacific deterrence has been hollowed out. The same CSIS report: PAC-3 and THAAD deliveries to Taiwan, Japan and Korea are now 18–36 months behind schedule. This is the most serious second-order consequence of the Iran war — within the 2026–2030 window, the probability of Chinese action against Taiwan rises 2–3 fold.

Under the maintained scenario the capacity build-out completes and inventory recovers; under the worst case demand outruns the expansion and shortages become structural.

FDD's Mark Dubowitz in a December 2025 report concedes Iran can rebuild missile production to 2,000–3,000 units/year within six months — meaning global demand for Iron Dome / PAC-3-class interceptors stays elevated even after the Iran ceasefire. The defense industry will not face a demand cliff.

The big three — Boeing, Lockheed Martin and Raytheon — have together booked over $200 billion in backlog during the same period, more than they can deliver across 2026–2028. Israel's Elbit, Rafael and IAI are seeing surging international orders — GCC states, Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Poland and South Korea are all buying Iron Dome-derived systems. Elbit's 2026 revenue is projected at $35 billion, 2.5 times its 2024 figure.

This is one of the most counterintuitive structural legacies of the Iran war — the conflict drained US munitions inventories but locked in 5–10 years of stable growth for the US-Israel defense industrial base.

chains.name: Semiconductors
chains.thesis: Israel's fab cluster (Intel Kiryat Gat, Tower Semiconductor Migdal HaEmek) is exposed to Hezbollah rocket fire; China's neon and krypton supply gives it leverage over global leading-edge production.
chains.current_baseline: Israel's fab cluster sits 60–180 km from the Lebanese border. Hezbollah's 130,000–150,000 rockets can saturate Iron Dome defenses (interception saturation around 1,000/hour).
chains.scenarios_impact: **Worst case** (Hezbollah "final strike"): at least one Israeli fab is directly damaged with a 3–5 year rebuild cycle; TSMC's 7-day LNG buffer is exhausted → Taiwan semiconductor output falls 60%+ → global AI chip supply disrupted 6–12 months
key_data: Intel Kiryat Gat sits in southern Israel, 80 km from the Gaza border
Tower Semiconductor Migdal HaEmek sits in northern Israel, 80 km from Lebanon
60% of global semiconductor neon comes from China + Ukraine
TSMC LNG buffer: 7 days / 90% gas imports / 90% via Hormuz
chains.analysis:
Israel's fab cluster is the single largest geographic vulnerability in the 2026 global supply chain. Intel Kiryat Gat (Foveros advanced packaging plus 14/10nm logic, roughly 15,000 employees) sits in southern Israel, 80 km from the Gaza border. Tower Semiconductor Migdal HaEmek (specialty analog and RF) sits in the north, 80 km from Lebanon. Iron Dome is capable but has a saturation point — estimated short-burst interception capacity is in the hundreds to ~1,000 per hour, constrained by Tamir interceptor inventory under sustained engagement.

Hezbollah is estimated to hold 130,000–150,000 rockets plus 50,000 precision-guided missiles (about 30% consumed during 2024–25). In a "final strike" saturation scenario, defenses can be breached within 6–12 hours. Once a fab is damaged, the rebuild cycle is 3–5 years — the semiconductor Lehman moment.

The second vulnerability is chemical inputs. 60% of global semiconductor neon comes from China plus Ukraine; the picture for krypton, xenon and ultra-high-purity neon is similar. Chinese export controls on these gases (already in place for gallium, germanium and rare earths since 2022–24) could expand at any time.

The third is TSMC's own exposure. TSMC's LNG buffer is 7 days, 90% of its gas is imported, and nearly all of that routes through Hormuz (Qatar). A Hormuz closure of 7+ days → Taiwan output down 60%+ → global AI training cycles delayed 6–12 months (dependent on NVIDIA H100 / H200 / B200 GPUs).

The response is the dispersal of Israel's fab cluster across Ohio, Arizona and Ireland — accelerated by the US CHIPS Act; partial Taiwan fab migration (TSMC Arizona, Kumamoto in Japan, Magdeburg in Germany); and Israeli national subsidies to maintain a domestic capacity floor. This is the 2026–2030 geographic redrawing of global semiconductor production.

chains.name: Financial plumbing
chains.thesis: A stress test of the sanctions architecture. E3 snapback, rising CIPS+mBridge share in the Global South, deeper SPFS-Bank Markazi integration — but Setser's argument that the dollar system holds at its core remains intact.
chains.current_baseline: The E3 (UK, France, Germany) triggered snapback in October 2025 — JCPOA's one-time right to unilaterally restore UN sanctions. Chinese CIPS cross-border payment volume jumped during the war; the Atlantic Council Toll Booth report documents 95% of Iran-China bilateral settlement clearing through mBridge in renminbi.
chains.scenarios_impact: **In all scenarios** the dollar system is stressed but not displaced — Setser's argument holds. **In the worst case** CIPS+mBridge captures 30%+ of non-dollar cross-border payments in the Global South, but at the cost of expanding PBoC balance-sheet risk
key_data: 95% of Iran-China bilateral settlement clears through mBridge (renminbi)
Setser's counterpoint: manufacturing Asia (China + Vietnam + South Korea + Japan) runs a $1.5 trillion trade surplus, dwarfing the $200B surplus of global oil exporters
Internal fissures inside BRICS+ — China-Saudi deepens, Russia and Iran lose weight

sources.label: CFR, Brad Setser, April 2026: Petrodollars — Myths and Reality
Atlantic Council, March 2026: Tehran's Toll Booth

chains.analysis:
The Iran war is a stress test of the sanctions architecture, operating at three layers.

First layer: traditional US OFAC (Office of Foreign Assets Control) secondary sanctions. Coverage spans all major Iranian banks and the key nodes of Iran-China oil and gas trade — Saudi storage facilities, STS (ship-to-ship) transfer points in Malaysia and Singapore. The "maximum pressure" framework Trump built after withdrawing from JCPOA in 2018 is now fully active.

Second layer: the E3 (United Kingdom, France, Germany) triggered snapback in October 2025 — JCPOA's one-time right to unilaterally reimpose UN sanctions. Iran is now under "full UN sanctions." The E3 triggered snapback specifically in October 2025 (rather than later) because Iran had already pushed its 60% enriched uranium stockpile close to weapons-grade — the core finding of IAEA report gov2025-50.

Third layer: real-world testing of alternative settlement systems. China's CIPS (Cross-Border Interbank Payment System) saw cross-border payment volume jump during the war. The Atlantic Council's Tehran's Toll Booth documents 95% of Iran-China bilateral settlement clearing through mBridge (the multi-CBDC bridge involving China, the UAE, Hong Kong, Thailand, Saudi Arabia and other central banks) in renminbi — the largest "around-SWIFT" case since mBridge entered operation. Russia's SPFS (System for Transfer of Financial Messages) has also deepened integration with the Central Bank of Iran.

But CFR's Brad Setser, in his April 2026 essay Petrodollars: Myths and Reality, pushes back hard: the claim that "Iranian oil settling in renminbi threatens the dollar system" is badly overstated. The logic: manufacturing Asia (China + Vietnam + South Korea + Japan) runs a combined trade surplus of $1.5 trillion, dwarfing the $200 billion surplus of all global oil exporters combined. Renminbi-settled oil does not displace the dollar at the foundation.

In the worst case, non-dollar settlement share globally rises from 5% to 30% — but at the cost of expanding PBoC balance-sheet risk. The cost of running CIPS / mBridge as "global monetary infrastructure" lands on the People's Bank of China.

hormuz_modes.name: Paid passage
hormuz_modes.thesis: The IRGC navy collects $2 million per ship as a "passage fee" but keeps shipping basically functional. The Atlantic Council's March 2026 Tehran's Toll Booth documents this mode in action.

sources.label: Atlantic Council, March 2026: Tehran's Toll Booth

hormuz_modes.analysis:
This is the operational pattern documented in Atlantic Council's March 2026 Tehran's Toll Booth. The IRGC navy neither blockades nor stands aside — it treats Hormuz as a toll gate. Each commercial vessel pays a tonnage-based fee and, once paid, sails through with basic order maintained. The report estimates an average $2 million per ship; at roughly 100 tankers per day, IRGC earns approximately $73 billion a year in passage fees — comparable to Iran's pre-war oil export revenue at 1.5 mb/d.

The mechanism at the core of this mode shifts "geographic rent" from "exporting our own oil" to "taxing other people's." The IRGC does not need Iranian central government authorization — the navy is itself a semi-autonomous IRGC command. Saudi Aramco's bypass plus the UAE's lines (a combined 4.7 mb/d of effective capacity) run at full load, but the spread the bypass cannot cover becomes IRGC profit.

Brent in this scenario returns to the $80–95 band — not back to the pre-war $70 level, but far below the wartime $120 peak. On shipping insurance, the Lloyd's JWC high-risk designation contracts from "the entire Persian Gulf" to "the Strait of Hormuz and Iranian coastal waters within 12 nm," and the private market gradually returns, though premiums stay 2–3× pre-war for at least five years. The 60× P&I cost spike that Hatzadony documents falls back to 8–12× under the maintained path.

Monitoring focus: weekly frequency of IRGC navy statements (currently 2–3/week; a drop to 1–2/month or internal contradictions would be an early signal of decentralization); daily ship-detention events in Hormuz (currently 0–1); actual records of per-ship tolls being collected (leaked shipping invoices would be key evidence).
hormuz_modes.subtitle: Toll-passage mode: $2 million per vessel keeps shipping basically functional
hormuz_modes.updated_subtitle_2026_05: **The dominant sub-state of the current mixed mode**: commercial vessels paying $2 million/ship to transit

hormuz_modes.name: Cyclical political coercion
hormuz_modes.thesis: The IRGC navy cyclically seizes, pressures and releases — turning passage into a bargaining chip. The 1987 Tanker War playbook returns.

sources.label: CRS R45281: Operation Earnest Will, 1987 historical precedent

hormuz_modes.analysis:
This is the 1987–88 Tanker War playbook in modern form. Back then the IRGC detained 67 commercial vessels in the Persian Gulf during the closing phase of the Iran-Iraq war — not closing Hormuz, just using ship seizures as political leverage. Under the militarization path Iran walks the same road: cyclical seizures, pressured negotiations, selective releases.

What separates this from "paid passage" is differentiated treatment. Higher probability of seizing US-flagged vessels, higher tolls on GCC tankers, duty-free transit for Chinese tankers — divide and rule. The IRGC sends Riyadh, Abu Dhabi and Doha different "cooperation asks" each month, forcing each to negotiate with Tehran individually rather than form a unified bloc.

Brent in this scenario fluctuates in $90–110. According to Quilliam's May 2026 Chatham House report, Saudi Aramco's plan to expand the East-West pipeline from 4 mb/d to 7 mb/d — pencilled in for later under the maintained path — gets accelerated to a 2027 completion. The UAE also fires up the second Jebel Dhanna-Fujairah line (1.5 mb/d).

China becomes the prime beneficiary. Through the shadow fleet plus duty-free transit, China's real discount on Iranian crude may deepen to $30–40/barrel below Brent — a discount no oil buyer has commanded since the Cold War. Meanwhile the US DFC's $40 billion reinsurance facility hardens into permanent institution; Setser's CFR tracking puts the DFC's risk exposure at $60 billion by April 2026, larger than all the agency's other business combined.

Monitoring focus: monthly ship-seizure events (currently 0–1; militarization moves this to 3–5/month with clear targeting); leaked IRGC differentiated pricing for ships of different flags; monthly DFC risk exposure disclosures.
hormuz_modes.subtitle: Selective ship detention as coercion: passage rights as bargaining chip, the 1987 Tanker War in modern form
hormuz_modes.updated_subtitle_2026_05: **Empirically demonstrated on 2026-03-18 at Ras Laffan**: cyclical seizures + selective releases as political coercion

hormuz_modes.name: Multilateral co-management
hormuz_modes.thesis: The Iranian central government loses effective control of the IRGC navy; Gulf states + the US + China maintain passage via de facto joint convoy escort. A more complex remake of 1987's Operation Earnest Will.
hormuz_modes.analysis:
This is Operation Earnest Will, version 2.0. In 1987 the US reflagged Kuwaiti tankers as US-flagged and escorted them through Hormuz with naval cover — but only for one country's ships. Multilateral co-management expands that mechanism into a de facto joint convoy involving GCC navies, the US Fifth Fleet, residual IRGC naval units and parts of the Chinese navy.

On the slow-collapse path, Tehran does not voluntarily cede control of Hormuz — its effective control erodes. Once any IRGC naval unit loses central command, Gulf states and the US step in to fill the vacuum, building informal arrangements to maintain traffic order. Counterintuitively, Brent falls back to $75–85, lower than under the maintained path — because risk premium is absorbed by the co-management mechanism and markets know there are layered insurers.

But slow-collapse has a "disorderly version" trap. If the IRGC will not voluntarily cede but cannot keep effective control, uncontrolled variables proliferate at sea — some days there's an arrangement, some days there isn't. Brent averages $85–100 but intraday volatility can reach $10. Shipping operators cannot plan.

The private insurance market returns in stages under multilateral co-management, with premiums falling from a 60× wartime P&I cost back to 5–8× pre-war over 3–5 years. The DFC facility is kept but trimmed to $10 billion of standby capacity. The new normal is "geographically tiered insurance" — inside the strait, central Persian Gulf, and Gulf of Oman priced separately, mirroring Lloyd's current treatment of the Red Sea.

Monitoring focus: Fifth Fleet joint convoy formation size (undisclosed today, would become public under co-management); GCC naval budgets (Saudi naval spending has already doubled from 2024 to 2026); DFC facility utilization falling from 80% to 20–30% would be a key signal.
hormuz_modes.subtitle: Joint convoy escort: after Iran loses control, the GCC, US and China jointly maintain the channel
hormuz_modes.updated_subtitle_2026_05: **Theoretically possible**: joint multilateral escort maintains transit after Iran's center loses control

hormuz_modes.name: Anarchic breakdown
hormuz_modes.thesis: IRGC naval units turn into privateers; command chains at Bandar Abbas, Bushehr and Chabahar break down. Khuzestan oil fields slip out of control. The private insurance market fully retreats.
hormuz_modes.insurance: Private market fully retreats; only sovereign-backed shipping operates
hormuz_modes.analysis:
This is the most dangerous, least likely scenario — roughly 3–5% probability within 24 months. But the damage envelope is disproportionate to that probability.

The mechanism is not blockade but disorder. After command chains break at the IRGC's three main naval bases (Bandar Abbas, Bushehr, Chabahar), provincial naval units operate autonomously — a Bandar Abbas unit might negotiate tacit passage arrangements with GCC counterparts; a Bushehr unit might turn privateer and levy private tolls; others might simply slip into piracy. Commercial shipping cannot plan around any of it.

More serious still is what happens if Khuzestan unrest reaches the East-West pipeline. Khuzestan holds 80% of Iran's oil reserves and is the home of the Ahwazi Arab minority. Sustained unrest there could engulf the Khorramshahr valve station at the eastern terminus of Saudi Aramco's East-West line — the immediate result being that the combined 4.7 mb/d Saudi + UAE bypass capacity drops to zero. Hormuz transit and the bypass go down together.

Brent spikes to $150–180 in the short term, but demand destruction pushes price back down to $100–120 within 6–12 months. The way back down is via shrinking global industrial output — recession kills demand, not supply improvement.

Private insurance fully fails for 6–18 months. The cause is not lost appetite (the LMA data shows 88% still willing) but the inability to price — without a central government, there is no model for the probability of a risk event. Lloyd's only historical precedent is the eight-year non-coverage of the Port of Beirut during the 1982–1990 Lebanese civil war. Under disintegration the entire Persian Gulf could enter that "pricing paused" state.

The secondary shock is simultaneous degradation of the Bab el-Mandeb — the Houthis exploit Iran's weakening to expand attacks. With both the Persian Gulf and Red Sea routes failing, Eurasian shipping is forced onto the Cape of Good Hope for an extended period, and the just-in-time architecture of global supply chains breaks structurally. TSMC's LNG buffer is just 7 days; if Qatari LNG disruption and Hormuz closure combine, TSMC begins reducing output after seven days. That is the scenario the semiconductor industry least wants to hear in May 2026.
hormuz_modes.subtitle: Maritime anarchy: command chains at the three IRGC bases break, naval units turn privateer
hormuz_modes.updated_subtitle_2026_05: **Most dangerous scenario, 3–5% probability**: command chains at IRGC naval bases break down

worst_case_timeline.events: Mojtaba succeeds, but Sistani is publicly silent or refuses to bless. Large-scale Arab unrest in Khuzestan and synchronized PJAK Kurdish action on the western border. At least one provincial IRGC commander publicly dissents. IRGC central authority loses coordinated control over local commands. Central Bank of Iran FX reserves run out; the rial breaks 1:150K. Hezbollah, sensing its supply line is about to be cut, opens its "final strike" decision window.

Hezbollah fires 50,000–80,000 rockets and drones at Israel (about half its stockpile), saturating defenses over 6–12 hours in multiple waves. Iron Dome saturates; hundreds of warheads land on Israeli soil. At least one of Intel Kiryat Gat or Tower Semiconductor Migdal HaEmek takes direct damage — fab rebuild cycle 3–5 years. Simultaneously the Houthis escalate strikes on Bab el-Mandeb shipping. Khuzestan oilfields slip out of control, removing ~1.5 mb/d from global supply. The IRGC navy's command chains at Bandar Abbas, Bushehr and Chabahar break; some vessels go privateer.

Oil spikes to $180–220 — exceeding the relative magnitude of 1973. Global marine insurance pricing fails; Persian Gulf and Red Sea transit falls to 5–10% of pre-war volume. TSMC's 7-day LNG buffer is exhausted → Taiwan semiconductor output drops 60%+ → global semiconductor shortage. AI training cycles slip 6–12 months. European winter gas crunch — 50% of Qatari LNG unavailable. Fertilizer prices spike as gas-based Middle Eastern plants go offline. Global wheat and corn trade is hit by the Red Sea closure.

Egypt's Sisi government enters fiscal collapse — Suez Canal revenue to zero, Gulf aid dries up, tourism collapses. Sisi faces an Assad-style impasse: a coup, removal by his own circle, or forced re-accommodation with the Muslim Brotherhood. Pakistan's Balochistan cross-border insurgency escalates — the largest territorial-integrity threat ever faced by a nuclear-armed state. Indian investment in Chabahar/INSTC goes to zero. Lebanon as a state finally fails. Multiple European governing coalitions (Germany, France, Italy, Netherlands) fall under combined energy shock + recession + migration pressure. Saudi Arabia's Vision 2030 enters "crisis survival mode."

Global GDP contracts 2.5–3.5% — comparable to the 2008 financial crisis plus the first wave of COVID. The dollar system is stressed but retains primacy (Setser's argument holds). China's CIPS+mBridge captures 30%+ of non-dollar cross-border payments in the Global South, but at the cost of an expanding PBoC balance sheet. India strategically realigns — deeper ties with the GCC instead of Iran/Russia, accelerated alignment with the United States (QUAD upgraded toward a quasi-military alliance). US strategic attention is forced to remain anchored in the Middle East — the Taiwan Strait window is hollowed out for 5–8 years. Israeli military hegemony peaks but at the cost of industrial hollowing. Internal fissures inside BRICS+ deepen.

sources.label: CFR Setser, April 2026: Petrodollars — Myths and Reality

countries.name: Pakistan
countries.thesis: The most dangerous country overall — highest probability of dual collapse. Triple economic pressure stacks onto triple political fragility.
economic_exposure: Gulf remittances ~$30B/year (8% of GDP), 4.5 million workers in the Gulf
100% crude import dependence; an oil spike instantly breaks the 2024 IMF $7B EFF program
FX reserves ~$9B in May 2026 (1.5 months of imports), depleted within 3 months
The China-Pakistan Economic Corridor (CPEC) Gwadar port project has stalled on Balochistan unrest
political_fragility: Civil-military standoff — Asim Munir vs PTI supporters has remained unresolved since the May 9, 2023 riots
Balochistan Liberation Army (BLA) activity escalating; an Iranian Baluch uprising would activate the BLA
Tehrik-i-Taliban Pakistan (TTP) is resurgent in KPK; attacks rose 73% YoY in 2024
countries.upheaval_scenarios:
Economic collapse triggers street protests (the 2022 Sri Lanka template) → military intervention → three possible outcomes:

- military coup (Munir takes over)
- military-PTI compromise, Imran Khan released
- state fracture (Balochistan slips effectively out of control)
countries.external_risk: **The most dangerous international spillover** — Pakistan effectively collapses → the Modi government uses the moment to adjust the Kashmir status quo → an India-Pakistan border conflict at the highest intensity since the 1999 Kargil War (a border conflict between two nuclear-armed states)
countries.analysis:
Pakistan is the most dangerous country overall. Three layers of economic pressure plus three layers of political fragility, on top of nuclear-armed-state status, make it the most likely flashpoint within the 6–12 month window.

On the economic side: Gulf remittances of ~$30B/year (8% of GDP, 4.5 million Gulf workers) are the backbone of Pakistan's current account. A move in oil from $70 to $180–220 instantly breaks the 2024 IMF $7B EFF (Extended Fund Facility) — every macro assumption in the EFF was built around $70–90 oil. FX reserves of about $9B in May 2026 (just 1.5 months of import cover) would be depleted within three months. At the same time the China-Pakistan Economic Corridor (CPEC) Gwadar port project has stalled on Balochistan unrest — China's single largest investment in Pakistan (a $60B commitment).

On the political side: Army Chief Asim Munir's standoff with PTI (Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf, Imran Khan's party) supporters has been unresolved since the May 9, 2023 riots; in the February 2024 elections PTI won the most votes but was pushed to the margins of a military-arranged coalition. The Balochistan Liberation Army (BLA) sustains its campaign — averaging 2–3 attacks per month on CPEC projects since 2024. In the northwest, Tehrik-i-Taliban Pakistan (TTP) is resurgent in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa (KPK), with attacks up 73% YoY in 2024 (PICSS, the Pak Institute for Conflict and Security Studies).

The shape of upheaval: economic collapse triggers street protests (a Sri Lanka, July 2022 Gotabaya Rajapaksa template) → military intervention → three possible outcomes:

- a military coup (Munir takes over directly, à la Musharraf 1999)
- a military-PTI compromise, Imran Khan released and brought back in
- state fracture — Balochistan slips effectively out of control, KPK enters TTP quasi-governance

The most dangerous international spillover is India's response. The Modi government may use the moment to push the Kashmir status quo further (the second move after the 2019 abrogation of Article 35A). An India-Pakistan border conflict in 2027 could reach the highest intensity since the 1999 Kargil War — a border clash between two nuclear-armed states is the most dangerous tail of this transmission chain.

sources.label: Stimson 2026: Iran-aligned militias threaten Iraq and the Gulf

countries.updated_analysis_2026_05:
**Actual situation**: in 2026-04 the Shehbaz Sharif government publicly offered to be the 'Iran-Saudi mediator,' but the substantive base is domestic political theater.

**Domestic constraints**:
- The economy has endured ~80% USD shortage for two consecutive years
- IMF bailout negotiations have repeatedly collapsed
- Imran Khan remains jailed but the PTI party continues street mobilization
- Sharif needs an 'international influence' narrative to maintain legitimacy

**Limited leverage**: Pakistan's substantive leverage over Iran is limited. The Pak-Iran border Balochistan province had 3 cross-border armed conflicts in the past 18 months: 2024-01 Iranian missiles struck across the border, 2024-01 Pakistan returned fire, 2025-09 Jaish ul-Adl militant attack.

**Actual role**: Pakistan is more likely to play 'a face-saving venue for dialogue' than the role of substantive architect.

countries.name: Egypt
countries.thesis: Second-highest probability of political-economic dual collapse — Suez Canal, tourism, Gulf remittances and energy subsidies all under pressure simultaneously.
economic_exposure: Suez Canal revenue to zero (normal $900M–1B/month in 2023, already halved to $400–500M by 2024)
Tourism is 12% of GDP; already down 40% in 2024 on Gaza war, regional conflict knocks another 60%
Gulf remittances ~$32B/year (7–8% of GDP)
Fuel subsidies (8% of fiscal spending) cannot absorb the oil + LNG spike
political_fragility: Sisi has ruled by authoritarian means since the 2013 coup; the 2024 currency devaluation (Egyptian pound 1:30 → 1:50) has triggered shortages
The Muslim Brotherhood is designated a terrorist organization but its grassroots network persists
Internal factions within the military — Sisi personally vs the military as institution (30–40% of GDP, vast commercial empire)
Sustained pressure on the 1979 Camp David Accords with Israel from the Gaza war
countries.upheaval_scenarios:
Fuel subsidy cuts trigger bread riots (the 1977 Sadat-era template) → protests larger than the eve of Morsi's 2013 ouster → three outcomes:

- internal military coup replaces Sisi
- the vice president takes over but loses control
- Muslim Brotherhood political return
countries.external_risk: Shocks ripple to Israel's southern border (Rafah crossing management vacuum + Gaza humanitarian crisis intensification + space for ISIS-affiliated militancy in Sinai)
countries.analysis:
Egypt has the second-highest probability of political-economic dual collapse. Four economic pressures stack onto four political vulnerabilities; the collapse risk in the 6–12 month window is second only to Pakistan.

The four economic pressures:

- Suez Canal revenue collapses — from a normal $900M–1B/month in 2023 to $400–500M in 2024 after Red Sea attacks; combined Red Sea + Hormuz closure drives revenue to near zero
- Tourism collapses — 12% of GDP, already down 40% in 2024 from the Gaza war; full regional conflict knocks another 60% off
- Gulf remittances ~$32B/year (7–8% of GDP), chiefly from Saudi Arabia and the UAE
- Energy imports — Egypt became a net gas importer in 2022 and is now deeply dependent on Hormuz LNG; rising oil and LNG prices make the fuel subsidy system (8% of fiscal spending) unaffordable

The four political vulnerabilities:

- Sisi has held power since the 2013 coup, but the foundation is brittle — the 2024 devaluation (Egyptian pound from 1:30 to 1:50) has already produced sporadic street unrest over shortages
- the Muslim Brotherhood is designated a terrorist organization but the grassroots network persists
- internal military factions — tension between Sisi personally and the military as an institution (30–40% of GDP, a vast commercial empire)
- the Gaza war continues to strain the 1979 Camp David Accords with Israel

Upheaval form: fuel subsidy cuts trigger bread riots (the 1977 Sadat template) → protests larger than the eve of Morsi's 2013 ouster → three outcomes:

- an internal military coup replaces Sisi
- power passes to the vice president but slips out of his hands
- pressure for a Muslim Brotherhood return becomes acute

International spillover: shocks reach Israel's southern border (Rafah management vacuum + Gaza humanitarian crisis intensification + space for ISIS-affiliated militancy in Sinai). The risk profile is second only to Pakistan.

countries.name: Sri Lanka
countries.thesis: The memory of 2022 is fresh, and the structural fragility is almost a repeat.
economic_exposure: Crude + refined products + coal account for ~25% of imports
Gulf remittances ~$6B/year (7% of GDP)
Tourism is 12% of GDP
political_fragility: The leftist NPP government elected in November 2024 has limited economic-management experience
The memory of 2022 leaves the public with very low tolerance for a second deterioration
countries.upheaval_scenarios: Another FX crisis → street protests go straight to the 2022 mode (storming the presidential palace) → government falls / military steps in / fresh elections — but without the Indian-and-IMF emergency package this time (India is preoccupied with Pakistan, IMF resources are spread thin)
countries.analysis:
Sri Lanka's memory is still raw — between April and July 2022, FX reserves collapsed, inflation topped 70%+, fuel was rationed, and protesters stormed the presidential palace before Gotabaya Rajapaksa fled. The 2026 worst-case scenario is structurally almost a rerun.

Economic exposure:

- completely import-dependent on energy — crude, refined products and coal are ~25% of imports
- Gulf remittances ~$6B/year (7% of GDP) — any sharp drop hits reserves directly
- tourism (12% of GDP) collapses again

Political fragility: the leftist NPP (National People's Power), elected in September 2024 and led by Anura Kumara Dissanayake from the JVP (Janatha Vimukthi Peramuna, People's Liberation Front), has limited economic-management experience. The public memory of 2022 leaves no tolerance for a second deterioration.

Upheaval form: another FX crisis → street protests go straight to the 2022 mode → government falls / military steps in / fresh elections. Unlike 2022, this time there is no emergency Indian-plus-IMF package (India is preoccupied with Pakistan, IMF resources are spread among other crisis countries). The recovery path looks more like a 1980s Uruguay-style "lost decade."

The IMF gave Sri Lanka $3B in March 2023; the NPP government took office in November 2024 and continued the program. But the IMF's early-2026 emergency lending designs assume Brent stays below $100 — under cyclical coercion or disintegration scenarios Brent breaks $100 and IMF program pressure rises sharply.

Monitoring signals: monthly Central Bank of Sri Lanka FX reserve disclosure (~$7B in December 2025, 3.5 months of import cover); IMF Article IV consultation reports; monthly Modi-government aid figures to Sri Lanka.

A 2.0 revolution will not have the Gotabaya-style "storming the palace" form — the NPP has been in office only 14 months and the public will give it longer before tolerance breaks. But if inflation returns to 50%+ for six months or more, street mobilization could move even faster than 2022.

countries.name: Bangladesh
countries.thesis: Still in the unsteady transition that began with Sheikh Hasina's August 2024 ouster.
economic_exposure: Gulf remittances ~$22B/year (5% of GDP)
Ready-Made Garments (RMG) is 84% of exports, 11% of GDP
political_fragility: The Yunus interim government has no electoral mandate
BNP and Awami League remnants are both waiting for their moment
The military is watching closely (a long coup history: 1975, 1981, 1982, 1990)
countries.upheaval_scenarios: RMG layoffs → urban slum protests → Yunus government fails to stabilize → military intervention / chaotic elections / escalation along the Myanmar border (Rohingya crisis)
countries.analysis:
Bangladesh is still in the unsteady political transition that began with Sheikh Hasina's August 2024 ouster and the installation of the Muhammad Yunus interim government.

Economic exposure:

- Gulf remittances ~$22B/year (5% of GDP)
- the Ready-Made Garments (RMG) sector is 84% of exports and 11% of GDP — end demand is Western, but operations depend on energy and cotton imports
- an oil spike plus marine insurance pricing failure hits RMG export competitiveness directly

Political fragility: the Yunus interim government has no electoral mandate (Yunus, the 2006 Nobel Peace Prize laureate, was invited in as a technocratic transitional figure). The BNP (Bangladesh Nationalist Party) and Awami League remnants are both waiting for their moment. The military watches closely — Bangladesh has a long coup history (1975, 1981, 1982, 1990).

Upheaval form: RMG layoffs → urban slum protests → Yunus government cannot stabilize → military steps in or elections turn chaotic.

Spillover risk: escalation along the Myanmar border (the Rohingya crisis intensifies) — Bangladesh hosts roughly 1 million Rohingya refugees in camps already under sustained economic strain.

The stability foundation of the Yunus interim government is Western aid + Indian cooperation + a $4.7B IMF package. Hasina remains in exile in India and may return after a power transition — itself the largest variable in the India-Bangladesh relationship.

If the Strait control state worsens, Bangladesh's RMG exports (about $35B/year, the country's main FX earner) take a double hit: rising shipping insurance costs plus weakening Western demand. Order cuts from Walmart, Zara and H&M flow directly to 3.5 million RMG workers.

Monitoring signals: monthly Central Bank of Bangladesh FX reserves (~$22B in December 2025, 4 months of imports); Yunus government approval ratings; public Indian government posture toward Yunus; preparation for the 2027 elections. The political legitimacy window for the Yunus government is narrowing.

countries.name: Jordan
countries.thesis: The Hashemite monarchy looks stable but carries structural weaknesses.
economic_exposure: Gulf aid ~$3–4B/year (7–9% of GDP)
Energy is 95% imported
Tourism is 11% of GDP
political_fragility: Palestinian-origin population is 60%+ of the total and reacts strongly to the Gaza war
King Abdullah II's legitimacy rests on "maintaining regional balance"
The Muslim Brotherhood's Islamic Action Front (IAF) took 31 seats in the September 2024 election — the largest single bloc in parliament
countries.upheaval_scenarios: A repeat of the 2011 Arab Spring template — pressure for "constitutional monarchy reform," effective erosion of the 1994 peace treaty with Israel
countries.analysis:
Jordan's Hashemite monarchy looks stable but carries structural weaknesses.

Economic exposure:

- Gulf aid of $3–4B/year (7–9% of GDP) is political subsidy — it dries up when Gulf states have their own fiscal crises
- energy is 95% imported (Iraq pipelines + Egyptian gas); an oil and LNG spike makes fuel subsidies unsustainable
- tourism (Petra, the Dead Sea) is 11% of GDP — regional conflict drives it to zero

Political fragility:

- Palestinian-origin population is 60%+ of the total and reacts strongly to the Gaza war
- King Abdullah II's legitimacy (since 1999) rests on "maintaining regional balance" — when the balance breaks, legitimacy loosens with it
- the Muslim Brotherhood's Islamic Action Front (IAF) is the largest opposition party in parliament, with 31 seats (of 138) won in September 2024 — the largest single bloc

Upheaval form: a repeat of the 2011 Arab Spring template (Jordan saw only limited protests back then but underlying discontent has accumulated since) — pressure for "constitutional monarchy reform," effective erosion of the 1994 peace treaty with Israel, friction with Israel over the Gaza humanitarian corridor.

The IAF took 31 seats in September 2024 (22.5% of 138), making it the largest party — 13 seats more than the monarchy-aligned governing party. This is the strongest Muslim Brotherhood parliamentary presence since Jordan restored parliamentary democracy in 1989.

King Abdullah II (since 1999) maintains rule through royal tradition + US aid + Gulf aid. But the Trump administration's MAGA aid cuts (US aid to Jordan dropping from $1.8B to about $1.2B in FY2026) plus Gulf states' own fiscal preoccupations remove two pillars from the monarchy's financial base simultaneously.

Monitoring signals: IAF parliamentary moves (especially proposals on the 1994 Jordan-Israel peace treaty); Central Bank of Jordan FX reserves; monthly Gulf aid figures to Jordan; royal public appearance frequency (Abdullah II's health is a taboo subject but appearance frequency is a defensible proxy).

countries.name: Turkey
countries.thesis: Energy imports + a fragile lira + the Kurdish question, all pressing at once.
economic_exposure: Energy imports (Russia + Azerbaijan + Iraq + Iran pipelines) — oil, gas and LNG combined run about $70B/year
The lira has already collapsed (2021 1:7 → 2026 1:36+); inflation 60%+
political_fragility: Erdoğan's AKP holds the executive completely but lost the March 2024 local elections (CHP swept Istanbul and Ankara)
The PKK issue will heat up again once the PJAK is activated by Iranian civil war
countries.upheaval_scenarios: Erdoğan further hardens authoritarianism / escalation against Kurdish militants (including further military action against the KRG in northern Iraq) / push for Turkish-Azeri-area independence inside Iran
countries.analysis:
Turkey faces triple pressure: energy + currency + the Kurdish question.

Economic exposure:

- energy imports (Russia + Azerbaijan + Iraq + Iran pipelines) — oil plus LNG run about $70B/year; an oil spike pressures the lira again
- The lira is in hyperinflationary collapse (2021 1:7 → 2026 1:36+), inflation 60%+
- Manufacturing exports depend on three markets — Gulf + North Africa + Europe — all contracting at once

Political fragility: Erdoğan's AKP (Adalet ve Kalkınma Partisi, Justice and Development Party) holds the executive completely but lost the March 2024 local elections — the CHP (Cumhuriyet Halk Partisi, Republican People's Party) swept Istanbul and Ankara, with urban middle-class frustration over economic management piling up. The Kurdistan Workers' Party (PKK) issue will heat up again once Iran's internal collapse activates the PJAK (Iranian Kurdish militants).

Upheaval form: Erdoğan hardens authoritarianism (cracks down on CHP, manipulates the judiciary) / escalation against Kurdish militants (including further military action against the KRG, the Kurdistan Regional Government in northern Iraq) / pushes for Turkish-Azeri-area independence inside Iran — the last option costs Erdoğan his Russia relationship (Russia has strategic interests in Azerbaijan).

The Erdoğan regime (since 2003, 22 years) faces dual pressure:

- economic — the lira has lost a cumulative 40% in 2024–2025, inflation runs above 50%, and a worsening Strait control state pushes energy import costs higher
- PKK military action intensifies — under an Iran disintegration scenario, instability in Iran's Kurdish regions lets the PKK expand operations into southeastern Turkey

The AKP's defeat in March 2024 local elections (CHP winning Istanbul and Ankara) was the worst urban-voter loss in 22 years of AKP rule. The 2028 presidential election finds Erdoğan at his term limit (under the 2017 constitutional amendments) — he can extend only via constitutional change or by transferring power within the AKP, but there is no consensus successor and AKP internal factional conflict could spill into the open in his final two years.

Monitoring signals: the lira rate (1:50 is a key threshold); Central Bank of Turkey FX reserves (~$48B in December 2025, but about $100B in hidden swap-line debt); PKK public action frequency; Erdoğan's relationship with the military (deeply purged after the 2016 coup attempt but factions remain).
countries.updated_analysis_2026_05:
**Current positioning**: the Erdogan (Turkey's president, AKP chairman) government's 2026-04 position on Iran is balanced at a new equilibrium.

**Dual non-alignment**:
- Will not publicly side with Iran. After Syria's Assad fell in 2024-12, Turkey shifted from cooperation to competition with Iranian proxy networks.
- Will not publicly side with Israel. Turkey's hardline Gaza narrative constrains Erdogan's domestic base.

**Real interests**:

1. Capturing overflow natural gas trade from the ME war — including the TurkStream pipeline (Russia-Turkey Black Sea subsea pipeline) and potential routes for Iran's South Pars gas via Turkey transit
2. Continuing to consolidate suppression of Kurdish armed groups in northern Syria

**Expected role**: across 2026-2027, Turkey's most likely role is 'passive beneficiary plus covert mediation attempts.'

countries.name: Japan
countries.risk_window: High-exposure stable state under accelerated pivot
countries.thesis: **No collapse, but the largest political pivot since 1945**. The biggest reorientation of Japan's national character since 1945 is already underway in 2025–2026; the worst case only compresses the timeline from 5–7 years to 2–3.
economic_exposure: Crude is 95% imported, 88% via Hormuz (Saudi 40% + UAE 35% + Qatar 8% + Kuwait 7%)
LNG: 8–12% from the Middle East (chiefly Qatar), nearly all via Hormuz
SPR + commercial mandatory reserves: 240 days of import equivalent (90 days at the national level)
Oil spike → crude import bill from $100B → $250–300B; yen from 1:160 to 1:200+
CPI inflation pushed from 2–3% to 5–7% — a structural shock to Japan's long-running deflationary psyche
political_fragility: **The Takaichi Sanae government** has been in office since October 21, 2025 — an LDP + Ishin coalition (Komeito left the coalition in October 2025, the first break since 1999)

Six Takaichi policy preferences: 3% GDP defense spending / large-scale nuclear restart / expansion of economic security / constitutional revision / China deterrence / loose monetary policy — the first five align closely with the worst-case scenario
Sole policy contradiction: Takaichi's dovishness on rates vs Bank of Japan Governor Ueda Kazuo's hawkish tilt → Ueda may resign early
countries.upheaval_scenarios:
Four acceleration paths open simultaneously:

- defense spending 3% of GDP pulled into FY2027
- Article 9 "interpretation revision 2.0" — MSDF special legislation for Hormuz
- nuclear pushed from 7–8% to 20–25%
- JOGMEC's US LNG long-term contracts complete the move away from the Middle East (Middle East share down from 88% to 60–65% over 5–7 years)
countries.external_risk: US relations — the "money (defense) for money (tariffs)" trade framework may be formalized. China relations — a two-track structure of economic cooperation plus military confrontation comes to the fore. India, Australia, Korea — QUAD upgraded to a quasi-military alliance, with Japan leading Indian Ocean naval cooperation.
countries.analysis:
Japan will not collapse, but it will undergo the largest political pivot since 1945 — and that pivot is already underway in 2025–2026. The worst case only compresses the timeline from 5–7 years to 2–3.

Prime Minister Takaichi Sanae took office on October 21, 2025, leading an LDP + Ishin coalition (Komeito exited in October 2025, the first break in the coalition since 1999). As of the May 19, 2026 baseline she has been in office about seven months — Japan's first female prime minister.

Six Takaichi policy preferences:

- defense spending above 2% of GDP, moving toward 3%
- large-scale post-Fukushima nuclear restart
- expansion of the economic security framework
- constitutional revision of Article 9
- explicit deterrence posture toward China
- loose monetary policy. The first five align tightly with the worst-case scenario — crisis would let her achieve agenda items that would otherwise require politically painful pushes. Only the sixth (monetary policy) directly conflicts with the inflation shock; this is the largest internal contradiction in the Takaichi cabinet, with Bank of Japan Governor Ueda Kazuo leaning hawkish against her dovish stance

Four acceleration paths:

- defense spending at 3% of GDP gets pulled in from "within five years" to FY2027 — Japan already hit the 2% target ahead of schedule in FY2025
- Article 9 "interpretation revision 2.0" via MSDF (Maritime Self-Defense Force) special legislation on Hormuz
- nuclear pushed from 7–8% to 20–25% — the Takaichi government has already in early 2026 affirmed nuclear as a "dual necessity for decarbonization and energy security"
- JOGMEC's (Japan Oil, Gas and Metals National Corporation) US LNG long-term contracts move Middle East share down from 88% to 60–65% over 5–7 years

Nine observation indicators: yen/USD breaking 1:200; CPI above 6%; Takaichi cabinet approval below 30%; official announcement that 3% GDP defense spending is pulled into FY2027; SDF Hormuz special legislation submitted to the Diet; Komeito returning to the coalition on specific issues; Ueda Kazuo resigning early; restart decisions from the Nuclear Regulation Authority on batch reactor restarts; JOGMEC US LNG long-term contracts signed. Any four firing simultaneously confirms Japan has entered the acceleration phase of its largest postwar political pivot.

countries.name: Philippines
countries.risk_window: Most dangerous in Southeast Asia (12–18 months)
countries.thesis: The most dangerous country in Southeast Asia — structurally comparable to Bangladesh but with deeper political polarization.
economic_exposure: OFWs (Overseas Filipino Workers) in the Gulf number 2.2–2.4 million, sending home $13–15B/year (out of $37B in total remittances, 8.5% of GDP)
100% crude import dependence
Semiconductor back-end (OSAT) is about 7% of Philippine exports; a Taiwan fab slowdown hits 350,000 jobs
political_fragility: Marcos Jr. vs the Duterte family in open rupture — Sara Duterte was impeached in 2024
Rodrigo Duterte was arrested by the ICC on crimes against humanity charges in March 2025
MILF Bangsamoro transition + NPA remnants + South China Sea tensions with China
countries.upheaval_scenarios: Gulf remittances drop + oil spike → middle-class consumption contracts + OFW family income breaks → anti-Marcos street protests → Marcos relies on military repression or steps down early
countries.external_risk: China could exploit the moment to press on Second Thomas Shoal and other disputed waters
countries.analysis:
The Philippines is the most dangerous country in Southeast Asia — structurally comparable to Bangladesh but with deeper political polarization.

Triple economic exposure:

- OFWs (Overseas Filipino Workers) total about 10 million, with 2.2–2.4 million in the Gulf (Saudi Arabia, UAE, Qatar, Kuwait); Gulf-sourced remittances are $13–15B/year (out of $37B in total remittances, 8.5% of GDP)
- 100% crude import dependence — an oil spike transmits directly to ordinary citizens via LRT fares, taxi rates and fishing-fleet diesel; the 2018 oil spike alone dropped Duterte's approval by 8 points in the short term
- semiconductor back-end (OSAT, Outsourced Semiconductor Assembly and Test) is about 7% of exports; a Taiwan fab slowdown hits Philippine back-end employment (~350,000 jobs)

Political fragility: Marcos Jr. (Bongbong Marcos Jr.) allied with the Duterte family in 2022 (Sara Duterte as vice president) to win the election, but the two families openly broke in the second half of 2024 — Sara Duterte was impeached (the second VP impeachment in Philippine history); former president Rodrigo Duterte was arrested by the International Criminal Court (ICC) in March 2025 on crimes against humanity charges; in the May 2025 midterm elections the Duterte faction recovered Senate seats. Open warfare between two political dynasties + the Bangsamoro autonomous region transition (MILF, Moro Islamic Liberation Front) + NPA (New People's Army) remnants + South China Sea tensions with China all overlap.

Upheaval form: Gulf remittances drop + oil spike → middle-class consumption contracts + OFW family income breaks → anti-Marcos street protests (organized by the Duterte faction) → Marcos either leans on military repression (restarting the Duterte-era "drug war" model but with political opponents as targets) or steps down early.

Additional risk: China judges the South China Sea dispute window favorable, presses on Second Thomas Shoal and other contested features, and Manila is forced to pick between internal crisis and external conflict.

countries.name: Indonesia
countries.risk_window: Largest Southeast Asian economy at risk (18–24 months)
countries.thesis: The vulnerability is Pertamina's fuel subsidy system — in the worst case subsidies balloon to $60–70B, completely beyond what the budget can absorb.
economic_exposure: Pertamina fuel subsidies ~$28B in 2024 (2.3% of GDP); worst case $60–70B
1.5 million Gulf workers, $8–9B/year in remittances (0.6% of GDP)
LNG exports a short-term tailwind (~$7B/year)
political_fragility: Prabowo is a former Suharto-era general, Suharto's son-in-law, expelled by East Timor forces over the 1998 kidnapping of activists
Prabowo needs economic results to sustain approval, but the rupiah is already on a depreciation path
countries.upheaval_scenarios: Fuel subsidy reform → student-led protests → Prabowo caught in his "father's trap" — either repress (losing democratic standing) or abandon reform (fiscal collapse). High risk of a 1998 krismon rerun
countries.analysis:
Indonesia is Southeast Asia's largest economy (GDP $1.4 trillion, population 280 million), but the vulnerability is not GDP scale — it is the Pertamina (national oil company) fuel subsidy system.

Economic exposure:

- net oil importer (since 2004; current imports about 700,000 b/d) — a move in oil from $70 to $180–220 would expand Pertamina's 2024 fuel subsidy from ~$28B (12% of fiscal spending, 2.3% of GDP) to $60–70B, completely beyond what the budget can absorb
- Indonesian workers in the Gulf number about 1.5 million (mainly Saudi domestic workers + UAE construction workers), sending home $8–9B/year (0.6% of GDP)
- LNG is an Indonesian export (about $7B/year, chiefly to Japan, Korea and China) — a price spike is a short-term tailwind, but medium term Indonesia faces shrinking global demand

Political fragility: Prabowo Subianto took office in October 2024. He is a former Suharto-era general, Suharto's son-in-law, and was expelled by East Timor forces for kidnapping pro-democracy activists in 1998 — his authoritarian instincts sit on a fragile legitimacy base. Prabowo needs economic results to sustain support, but he inherited a rupiah already on a depreciation path, and the industrial-upgrade promise tied to copper and nickel export controls needs 5–10 years to bear fruit.

Upheaval form: if forced to cut fuel subsidies in 2025–2026, this rehearses the 1998 "krismon" (kris monetier, financial crisis) that toppled Suharto — back then oil spikes plus the Asian financial crisis combined; Jakarta fuel subsidy cuts triggered nationwide riots, 1,300 dead in three weeks, Suharto out. Prabowo's bloodline to Suharto makes this Indonesia's most politically sensitive nerve.

Spillover risk: effective erosion of the 2005 Aceh peace agreement, with the Aceh independence movement (GAM, Gerakan Aceh Merdeka) reactivating, and intensification of the West Papua independence movement.

countries.name: Thailand
countries.thesis: Chronic political fragility layered with supply chain shocks.
economic_exposure: Crude is 90% imported, LNG heavily dependent on Qatar
Tourism (12% of GDP) has only recovered to 75% of pre-COVID levels
Semiconductor back-end + auto parts depend on global demand
political_fragility: MFP won the most votes in 2023 but was blocked from forming a government; dissolved in 2024 into the People's Party
Pheu Thai + military + conservative coalition is internally tense
King Rama X succession is taboo (lèse-majesté Article 112)
countries.upheaval_scenarios: Economic shock + tourism contraction → Pheu Thai loses economic-management legitimacy → repeat of 2006/2014 coup template, or 2010 Red Shirt street politics
countries.analysis:
Thailand is chronic political fragility layered with supply chain shock.

Economic exposure:

- crude 90% imported, LNG heavily dependent on Qatar (~$10B/year of imports) — an oil and gas spike hits industrial power costs directly
- tourism (12% of GDP) has only just recovered to 75% of pre-COVID; full regional conflict drops visitor numbers another 30–50% (especially Middle East + India + China)
- semiconductor back-end (as with the Philippines) + auto parts (Toyota, Honda, Ford all have major production in Thailand) depend on global demand

Political fragility: in the 2023 election the anti-establishment Move Forward Party (MFP) won the most votes but was blocked from forming government; the eventual coalition was Pheu Thai + the military + conservatives, with high internal tension; Thaksin Shinawatra returned in 2023 and secured judicial protection but with diluted effective power; in 2024 the Constitutional Court dissolved MFP, with its successor People's Party inheriting seats and remaining the largest opposition, but the dissolution threat is not fully lifted; most sensitive is the royal succession — King Rama X's (Maha Vajiralongkorn) health remains a taboo topic (lèse-majesté Article 112), and any activation of the succession question immediately becomes a political fault line.

Upheaval form: economic shock + tourism contraction → Pheu Thai coalition loses economic-management legitimacy → both the anti-establishment Move Forward camp and the military try to fill the vacuum → either a repeat of the 2006/2014 coup template, or in the other direction something resembling the 2010 Red Shirt street politics.

Special risk: this is one of the most fragile windows for democratic constitutionalism since 1932, layered with royal-succession uncertainty. A 1957 Sarit Thanarat-style military takeover could re-emerge in 2026–2027.

countries.name: Myanmar
countries.thesis: Already in full-scale civil war following the February 2021 coup. The Iran war effect is to accelerate an existing collapse.
economic_exposure: Gas exports (Yunnan-Myanmar-Indian Ocean pipeline) bring $3B/year — the junta's main FX source
Black-market diesel prices broke 3× the official price by early 2026
Chinese support for the junta hinges on pipeline security; the October 2023 Three Brotherhood Alliance attacks made Beijing reassess investment
political_fragility: Effective SAC junta territory has shrunk from 70% to 50–55%
Three Brotherhood Alliance (MNDAA + TNLA + AA) + NUG/PDF keep advancing
Unstable generational handover between Min Aung Hlaing and the next tier (Soe Win and others)
countries.upheaval_scenarios:
Three possibilities:

- the junta accepts the ASEAN Five-Point Consensus and opens a transition (least likely)
- the junta dissolves but no unified successor → Libya-style fragmentation
- the junta hardens its grip on core regions and abandons the periphery. Refugee outflows push into Thailand (Chiang Mai/Chiang Rai) and Bangladesh (Rohingya camps already overloaded)
countries.analysis:
Myanmar has been in full-scale civil war since the February 2021 coup (the SAC, State Administration Council junta against the NUG, National Unity Government, plus the ethnic armed organizations, EAOs). The Iran war effect on Myanmar is to accelerate ongoing collapse rather than trigger a new one.

Economic exposure:

- gas exports (mainly via pipelines to China + Thailand, ~$3B/year) are the junta's main FX source — rising global LNG prices are a short-term tailwind for the junta
- refined product imports (diesel + gasoline) — oil price spikes have pushed black-market prices to 3× the official price by early 2026, putting them beyond ordinary citizens
- Chinese support for the junta hinges on the security of the Yunnan-Myanmar-Indian Ocean oil and gas pipelines, and the Three Brotherhood Alliance's pressure along that line since the October 2023 "1027 Operation" has forced Beijing to reassess investment

Political fragility: SAC effective territorial control has shrunk from about 70% to 50–55% since 2023 (UN estimate); three EAOs (Kokang MNDAA, Ta'ang TNLA, Arakan AA) plus NUG-affiliated People's Defense Forces (PDF) continue to advance; Aung San Suu Kyi (Aung San Suu Kyi) remains in prison at age 80 (as of mid-2025) with health concealed; within the military, generational handover between Min Aung Hlaing and the next tier (Soe Win and others) is unstable.

Upheaval form: oil prices + black-market economy + Chinese investment in wait-and-see → junta-controlled territory shrinks further → three possibilities:

- the junta accepts the ASEAN Five-Point Consensus (5PC), opens a transition (least likely)
- the junta dissolves, but with no unified successor force enters "Libya-style" fragmentation (no central government)
- the junta hardens its grip on core regions (Yangon, Naypyidaw, Mandalay) and abandons the periphery

Spillover risk: refugee flows — about 2 million internally displaced people inside Myanmar today, further collapse pushes refugees into Thailand (Chiang Mai/Chiang Rai already absorbing them) and Bangladesh (Rohingya camps already overloaded).

cross_chain_feedback.name: Semiconductors → defense
cross_chain_feedback.description: Israeli fabs damaged → global leading-edge capacity down 30% → tight supply of precision GaN chips for Patriot / THAAD / Iron Dome interceptors → US weapons deliveries to Taiwan, Japan and Korea slip 18–36 months

cross_chain_feedback.name: Oil + shipping + insurance → semiconductors
cross_chain_feedback.description: Oil spike + marine insurance fails → cross-ocean transport costs for chemicals (neon, krypton) up 5× → inventory pressure at TSMC + Samsung + SK Hynix → global leading-edge output down 15–30%

cross_chain_feedback.name: Insurance → everything
cross_chain_feedback.description: Private insurance market retreats → no operator will assume uninsured shipping risk → Persian Gulf + Red Sea transit falls to 5–10% of pre-war volume → simultaneously hits oil & gas / fertilizer / semiconductors / food trade

cross_chain_feedback.name: Defense industry → Indo-Pacific deterrence
cross_chain_feedback.description: US arms industry capacity expansion 100% routed to the Middle East → PAC-3 / THAAD / GMD inventories for the Taiwan theater are emptied → probability of Chinese action against Taiwan rises 2–3 fold

cross_chain_feedback.name: Critical minerals → everything
cross_chain_feedback.description: Chinese export controls on gallium, germanium and rare earths → semiconductors + defense + clean energy hit simultaneously → US / Japan / EU activate critical-mineral emergency reserves

cross_chain_feedback.name: Finance → everything
cross_chain_feedback.description: OFAC sanctions + E3 snapback → all major Iranian banks cut off from SWIFT → 95% of Iran-China bilateral settlement runs through mBridge → other oil exporters (Russia, Venezuela, Iraq) watch to see whether to follow → global non-dollar settlement share rises from 5% to 30%

history_compare.title: 1973 vs 2026: structural contrast of two oil crises
history_compare.intro: The 1973 oil shock was the most consequential global supply chain event of the late twentieth century. Set against the 2026 Iran war along four dimensions:

scenarios.label: OPEC Arab members impose embargo on states supporting Israel
scenarios.narrative: The first oil crisis of 1973 — OPEC Arab members embargoed states supporting Israel. Key numbers: crude rose from $3 to $12/barrel (4×); duration five months (October 1973 to March 1974); OECD GDP fell 0.6% in 1974; equity markets dropped ~40%; inflation moved from 4% to 11%. Structural consequences: Japan and Europe launched conservation policies; North Sea + Alaska + Gulf of Mexico exploration accelerated; the IEA (International Energy Agency) was founded in 1974; the US SPR (Strategic Petroleum Reserve) launched in 1975.
scenarios.applicability: **Partial fit**. On scale, 1973 saw a 4× price move, 2026 is 1.7× ($70 → $120); 2026 is less severe (so long as the worst case doesn't crystallize). On duration, 1973 had a clean five-month arc, 2026 has dragged on 11 months with no resolution — more of a "long tail."

scenarios.label: Geographic chokepoint + geopolitical conflict + cross-chain feedback stacked
scenarios.narrative: The 2026 Iran war can be set against the 1973 crisis along four dimensions. First, scale — 1973 was 4×, 2026 is 1.7×. Second, duration — 1973 had a clean five-month arc, 2026 is in its eleventh month and recurring. Third, mechanism — 1973 was an OPEC political embargo (supply-side policy), 2026 is geographic chokepoint control + geopolitical conflict + cross-chain feedback (multiple mechanisms stacked). Fourth, financial impact — 1973 mostly transmitted through the oil price, 2026 also transmits through insurance market failure, semiconductor supply chain dispersal, defense industry capacity crowding and critical mineral leverage — four independent channels. This is a full-value-chain shock.
scenarios.applicability:
**Key structural difference**: the structural legacies of 1973 were the Western completion of "oil-non-dollar settlement" (Saudi agreement to dollar oil settlement), North Sea exploration and Japanese automotive global expansion. The structural legacies of 2026 may be:

- private insurance permanently retreats from geopolitical hotspots, sovereign backstops become standard
- the US defense industry completes capacity expansion, but at the cost of hollowed-out Indo-Pacific deterrence through 2026–2030
- Israel's fab cluster disperses across Ohio + Arizona + Ireland
- China's CIPS+mBridge completes renminbi-settlement coverage of oil exporters
- critical mineral supply chains regionalize

history_compare.current_judgment: The danger to watch is not Iran's regime path itself, but how that path shapes the **geographic chokepoint operational mode** — and the **cross-chain feedback amplification** that follows once the operational mode slips out of control. Hormuz does not disappear; it is simply used in different ways. Every additional IRGC naval unit acting on its own, every additional ship sailing the Persian Gulf without coverage, every Patriot interceptor pulled from US stockpile to Israel, every Iran-China settlement cleared through mBridge, every semiconductor wafer relocated from Kiryat Gat to Ohio — these accumulate into a structural reset of global supply chains, from a pre-war "efficiency-maximizing" architecture to a post-war "resilience-first" one. This is a process that will unfold across the next six or seven years, 2026–2032.

glossary.note: Four possible states for Strait of Hormuz passage. The regime path sets the mode: maintained → toll-passage mode; militarization → cyclical political coercion; slow-collapse → joint convoy escort; disintegration → maritime anarchy. The geography does not change, only the way it is used.

Iran's IRGC turns Strait of Hormuz passage rights into political leverage — cyclical seizures, pressured negotiations and selective releases force each country to bargain with Tehran individually. Not a closure (that equals total war) — selective disruption. The IRGC's seizure of 67 vessels during the 1987–88 Tanker War is the historical precedent.
The only maritime route from the Persian Gulf to the Indian Ocean. About 30% of global seaborne oil and 20% of LNG transit. Narrowest width about 33 km.

glossary.zh: Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries
glossary.en: Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries
glossary.note: Oil-producer cartel founded 1960, currently 13 members. Imposed the embargo on supporters of Israel during the 1973 first oil crisis.

2015 multilateral agreement limiting Iran's nuclear program in exchange for sanctions relief. Trump withdrew in May 2018. The E3 (UK, France, Germany) triggered snapback in October 2025 — the agreement's one-time right to unilaterally restore UN sanctions.
Cross-border renminbi clearing system launched by the People's Bank of China in 2015; a potential SWIFT alternative. Cross-border payment volume jumped during the 2026 war.

Multi-central-bank CBDC settlement project involving China, the UAE, Hong Kong, Thailand, Saudi Arabia and others. Atlantic Council reporting puts 95% of Iran-China bilateral settlement through mBridge in renminbi.
National crude stockpile for supply disruption. US SPR launched 1975, Japan's in 1978.

US law passed during the 1950 Korean War authorizing the president to compel industrial conversion for defense. The 2026 expansion of PrSM / THAAD / Patriot capacity has been accelerated under DPA authority.
Iran's core armed force, with five services and 17 provincial corps. The navy is a separate command responsible for the Strait of Hormuz and the Persian Gulf.
US government overseas investment agency. In 2026 it leads the standing-up of a $40B sovereign reinsurance facility (per the WEF report) to absorb shipping risk after private insurance retreats.
Belt and Road flagship project; $60B in infrastructure investment in Pakistan, concentrated in Balochistan + Khyber. The Balochistan Liberation Army (BLA) has sustained attacks on CPEC projects.

causal_map.title: Transmission path: from Iran regime state to country political crisis
causal_map.intro: Iran's four regime paths transmit to global supply chain shocks through a five-step causal chain, and on to specific countries' political crises. This is a multi-level causal pathway.

items.name: Maintained
items.desc: 40–45% · Mojtaba succeeds smoothly + IRGC remains intact

items.name: Militarization
items.desc: 15–20% · IRGC sets aside religious legitimacy and takes over the state

items.name: Shipping / insurance failure
items.desc: Private insurance retreats; sovereign backstops become standard

items.name: Semiconductor output cuts
items.desc: TSMC LNG buffer exhausted → output down 60%+

items.name: Pakistan (6–12 months)
items.desc: Gulf remittances break + IMF program fails + India-Pakistan border conflict risk

items.name: Egypt (6–12 months)
items.desc: Suez + tourism + Gulf aid + fuel subsidies all under pressure

items.name: Indonesia (18–24 months)
items.desc: Pertamina fuel subsidy system vs Prabowo's 1998 trap

items.name: Sri Lanka / Bangladesh / Jordan / Turkey / Thailand / Myanmar (12–24 months)
items.desc: Specific mechanisms for each; see country matrix

items.name: Japan (no collapse, but accelerated pivot)
items.desc: Takaichi government → 3% defense / Article 9 / nuclear / pivot away from the Middle East

causal_map.current_assessment: **Current position**: at L1 "maintained" (40–45%) + L2 "paid passage" baseline. All five chains in L3 show pressure but have not entered crisis. L4 already shows early signals — Pakistan IMF program strain, Philippine OFW income decline, the Takaichi government accelerating its defense and nuclear agendas. **Key threshold**: if Iran's regime path falls from maintained into disintegration (Sistani publicly rejects Mojtaba, or the IRGC splits openly — either alone is enough), the entire causal chain steps down one rung — L2 enters "anarchic breakdown," L3 enters crisis, and at least 3–4 countries in L4 enter genuine political crisis. The cascade could complete within 6–12 months.

hubs.name: Iran regime → Strait control state
hubs.summary: Iran's four regime paths do not set global oil prices directly. They run through two intermediate variables — IRGC command coherence and naval ROE consistency — which together determine which of the four Strait operational modes prevails.
